text: The surgeon uses the da Vinci XI robotic system to perform a sleeve gastrectomy, with robotic arms positioned from the patient's right and left. The gastrosophia fat pad, located between the gastric lumen and the left crux of the diaphragm, is being removed using a robotic vessel sealing device. This device seals small blood vessels and cuts through the tissue, providing exposure of the angle of His for stomach dissection.

The surgeon has completely removed the gastroesophageal fat pad and is now focusing on the lower stomach. The pylorus is identified through palpation and visualization. The surgeon marks a point on the lesser curve of the stomach, approximately four to six centimeters proximal to the pylorus, to begin transecting the greater curve vessels. Using a vessel sealing device, the surgeon seals and cuts through the greater curve vessels, opening the lesser sac behind the stomach by cutting through the connective tissue.

The surgeon enters the posterior space and begins transecting the short gastric vessels along the greater curve of the stomach. The spleen is visible, and the vessels are closely situated between the stomach and spleen. The surgeon carefully continues the dissection to avoid bleeding, ensuring clear mobilization of the greater curve without bleeding in the splenic bed.

The surgeon cauterizes the final attachments on the posterior aspect of the stomach, ensuring clear visualization of the left crus of the diaphragm. This step frees the stomach, facilitating the placement of the stapling device for the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon passes a 34 French sizing tube down the esophagus into the antrum of the stomach under direct vision. Using a robotic stapling device, a large green stapler is applied to create the initial staple line in the thicker portion of the stomach. The robotic technology measures tissue thickness to ensure proper staple formation. The staple line is inspected, showing no bleeding or serosal tears, and the process is repeated vertically along the stomach.

The surgeon passes the final staple line across the apex of the stomach, leaving a small dog ear of stomach to ensure the staple line is on the stomach and not the esophagus, which is positioned to the left. The complete staple line of the gastric sleeve is inspected, and the surgeon uses a robotic needle holder to over-sew the staple line in a running inverting fashion, starting at the dog ear above the staple line.

The surgeon completes the over-sewing of the suture line for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy. The small dog-ear portion of the stomach is tacked to the left crux of the diaphragm using an interrupted vial suture to prevent herniation into the mediastinum.

The surgeon ties the final suture and removes it, followed by the removal of the thirty-four French bougie. An upper endoscopy is performed, with saline covering the area to check for air leaks. The endoscope light is visible as it passes through the distended gastric sleeve and antrum, confirming no air leaks.

The surgeon suctions residual air from the gastric sleeve and removes the endoscope. Fibrin sealant is applied to the suture line and a small serosal tear on the liver, effectively controlling the area without bleeding. The gastric specimen and liver retractor are removed, concluding the procedure.
